speaker: interviewer
text: three, August 10th, 1995. My name is Gloria Schneider. We are interviewing Matthew Guttman in Queens, New York. Mr. Guttman, you were talking about someone who was saying, [NON-ENGLISH] for his friend, and the German came over.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the German, the Gestapo came. The Gestapo says, what is with him? He says, he's singing, he's delirious? I said no. That is the prayer of dead. I said that is-- I says, his friend died during the night, and now he's giving him the last prayer. And that's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he went away. He left me just there. That was going on until around March-- the beginning of March, 1945. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was taken to the healthy barrack, and we were no more working.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day, we are coming out, they are hollering alarm, alarm, alarm. We are moving. We are going. And the microphone, whoever thinks that he can walk and  will stay in the line, he's going to get himself a bread. Because we are going away from here.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you think that you cannot walk, remain behind, because we're going to take you by truck. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I remained behind because I was only recuperating, really. I wasn't that healthy yet. I thought to myself, why should I walk? I should put myself in danger when I could go by truck? But when the people that stayed in the line, one of-- a friend of mine says to me, Matthew, what are you doing? Jump in the-- I says, why? I'm going to go by truck. Dead. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jump in the line.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were going already. I didn't get my bread, because I didn't stay in the line in the time. So anyway, I went, thanks to my friend. Because right away, as soon as we left from there, about, maybe, 500 meters, I heard the hollerings. I heard the cryings. The-- they were closed in in the barracks. All they remained in behind. They were locked into the barracks, and lit the fire, and the barracks were burning because they were all wood. The barracks were burning alive-- the people alive inside of there. I would've been one of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went. We started walking, a truck behind us. We were five people in a row. A thousand rows is 5,000 people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is the way we were going. I-- we didn't know where the SS-- the Gestapo-- they were taking us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wherever we stopped, first of all, the Allies came with the airplanes. They saw a bunch of people. They didn't know they were Army or civilians. They didn't know they are Jews. They were bombarding us. We had an order to lay down on the grass with the face down. I said to myself, the hells with you. I'm going to put myself with the face up. I want to see when the-- when the [INAUDIBLE] are laying the eggs. I thought to myself, I wish [NON-ENGLISH] one egg should fall on top of me. That's it. But it didn't. There were some killings, but it didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the airplanes just continued further. I was dreaming if they would let down a rope from there, from the airplane, and I should hang myself to the rope, and they should pull me up. My God. Those are the Allies. United States of America, England. I would be a free person.

speaker: interviewee
text: Impossible. We give it up. We didn't believe that this is even possible. So we went, a truck behind. They pulled up-- out from the bunch, six people behind the truck. And people who cannot walk no more, lay them on the grass, [INAUDIBLE], with a dum-dum bullet. They shot you from behind in the head, that the head-- a bunch-- a piece of your head fell away. It's a dum-dum. It explodes twice. Once when he releases from the gun, and when it comes in contact with the body, with the aim. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Those six people, they were carrying the dead bodies, what-- what were executed, and throw them on top of the truck. And this is the way it was going on, and we were getting the bread, what they found by the dead bodies. I was captured once in this bunch here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it passes by a transport of 5,000 people. Again, a friend of mine is there. He sees me, that I am behind the truck. I didn't know what was going on. He said to me, he says, Matthew, what are you doing there? Jump in the line.

speaker: interviewee
text: You couldn't jump in their line, and I can't because there were five in a row holding hands. You couldn't jump because you would have been the sixth one. They catch you, they kill you. But they were in places where they were missing. You know, they couldn't walk no more and they would kill them. So now they remain four. So I jumped in on one of these lines, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I went. These people, wherever we arrived that night, they-- we stopped. They had to dig a big ditch to bury the dead bodies from the truck, and then they were shot. There shouldn't remain a witnesses. And that's the way it was going down. Tomorrow morning another six people from the bunch taken out. And that's the way it started. It was-- places we stopped because it was an air raid. Again, the Allies came bombing. On the end of the transport, there were SS. They were aiming only for sport. And people, the same thing. Like they would aim at squirrels, or on rabbits, or whatever, and killing. People were running from behind. We stopped and they were running from there, because they were killing them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, it was a time when we started running the night-- at night [INAUDIBLE], only we couldn't stop no more. We had to run at night too. And on every Austrian village when we arrived, it looks like they let them know there's Jews arriving. They came they Hitler Jugend. Hitler Jugend means, in translated, that is the juveniles, the Hitler juveniles, so they were brought up, you know, as Hitlerites. They were waiting for us with machine guns. And they started shooting, cutting. And I was jumping over live ones, over wounded ones, over dead ones, only to get away from there. And everybody, not only me, we were running. You couldn't run away because first of all, you had, on the left hand side, on your breast, you had the yellow star of David, Jude. Even if you would wanted to take that off, you couldn't hide because, first of all, you looked like a concentration camp guy because you were skinny. You were a skeleton. Not-- they were not dead. They were [GERMAN]. They ate like horses. Everything what they were robbing, you know, from all the countries wherever they were. They were bringing everything into their father land. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So one day, they took us in the stone mine, where they were mining rocks, building highways. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was down. We had to walk down-- all the way down. Everyone, maybe a few thousand people. [INAUDIBLE] About 8,000 or 10,000 people. On the top, around and around, I'm looking around and around. The SS with the machine gun-- guns aimed at us. I said to myself, Matthew, here is the finale. I just wished myself that the bullet should finish me up. I shouldn't remain. This I should [NON-ENGLISH] myself. I should be wounded and should take a long time, my dying. Because sooner or later they came, and they controlled who's dead and who's alive. Who was still alive, they finished them off with the second bullet. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that happened, the [INAUDIBLE] would be a raid. It was during the night. The Allies came and they were bombarding the mine. The rocks were flying. Now you weren't afraid about bullets. You weren't afraid about bombs. The rocks were killing you. It was an avalanche of rocks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I went through that, though. They put us in the line. We went and marched further. Until they're taking us to some kind of carloads, in some kind of a dead line on railway tracks. And they have the Pullman wagons. I don't know if you know the Pullman wagons, the European Pullman wagons? They're luxury railroad cars.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they throw us in, into those cars, like sardines in a sardine can. 120 per car load. Up the top, where they used-- we used to put the luggage or anything like that. You know, by [INAUDIBLE] used to be people there, because they didn't have no place on the floor or whatever. Forget about the seats.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were carrying us. No food, no nothing. Until about three or four days, they-- they pushed us in a dead line on the field. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I'm looking around. I should've guessed, where are we? I see Lyon, France. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But we didn't see no civilian population. We didn't see nothing. They let us out of the car loads. They were opening up the-- the car loads and they let us out. They were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe 30 people from 120 alive, and the rest were-- only some were suffocated, were stepping one on top of the other one. Or dying from malnutrition or dying for hunger, or for-- they were to the end of the-- that was the finish line. Now here, they let us graze on the field. And believe it or not, we ate grass. If you had to go to [INAUDIBLE] necessities to go to the bathroom, there were no bathrooms. There were nothing at all. You had to ask permission. And there is-- you-- the law was 20 feet away from here. You can go and let down your pants and do your necessities. If-- there were people where they couldn't do it. You know, they were shy and they couldn't do it. They would go out a little bit further. The guard took his gun, [INAUDIBLE]. That's it. Finish him up. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One day-- one night, I hear a train is coming in. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No people, but [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] only car loads full of merchandise or something. In the dark, I'm stealing myself out. I see right near us there is a car load. I'm sticking my hand on top. I'm climbing on top. It was threshed wheat. But they didn't have not time to clean it, but it is threshed wheat. I'm taking off my jacket, I'm tying up my sleeves, and filling up my sleeves with wheat. I'm going-- coming in the car load and I'm starting to blow the wheat. I clean it up. I start eating it. It was good. You had some calories. We're eating. Then the other morning, I see big ditches. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't know what it-- what did that contains, because I knew something-- there is something there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You couldn't go in daytime prospecting. You weren't allowed. But, like I told you, 20 feet. That night in the dark, I stole myself out. Even they were tired too. And I'm going to the ditch there, and I starting digging, you know, with my hands. Turnips. My god, that was godsend. I'm bringing-- getting in the car load. They're bringing in turnips. The people are asking me where from. I says, hey man. We are going-- they are going to find out what we are doing and they are going to kill us. Go one at a time with watch. And a little bit we brought in. Finally, they are taking us away from here.

speaker: interviewer
text: But here, you were just living in a field.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the field, in the car load. Nothing doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was around end of March. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They're taking us now back-- they're taking us to a place called Mauthausen. Back to Austria because then, that time, Germany was squeezed like a lemon. From one side, Americans. The second side, the English. The third side, the French partisans, the La [? Maquis. ?] Or the fourth place, the Russian front.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were pushing the hell out of them. They were squeezing them like a lemon. And we didn't know, you know, where to zig zag. You know? [INAUDIBLE] The place was getting tighter, and tighter, and tighter. They didn't have anywhere to take you. So finally they took us to Mauthausen. In Mauthausen, they took us under the free sky, only it was surrounded with barbed wire and electric power on it. If you-- lots of people, they ran to the wire, and they remain hanging on the wire.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was dirt. It was raining. It was the spring. Marching-- by the way, marching through the woods-- I'm coming back now. I'm jumping from one place to the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. So now this is before you got to Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I got to Mauthausen, marching through the woods. I-- they were shooting, of course. I told you that. Those people who they couldn't go, they were shooting them. I see a person, a young man. He is leaned against a tree and crying out loud with a big hole in the head, here. You could see his brains. You could see his brains. He says, somebody shot me and they didn't kill me. Passes by an SS man. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was walking-- I didn't have no more shoes-- I was walking on my own feet soles. And it was still, you know, snow juice outside. It was not snow, but it was, you know, that cold, dirty, spring weather. Cold like hell. And I was-- I'm telling you, I was walking on my own soles. They're shooting the man and the man had a very-- they're coming from behind. He didn't even see.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came from behind, an SS man, and gave him a bullet in the head. And behind him, he fell down. And that's all to it. And I see that he has a pair of brand new Army boots. I said to myself, my God, Matthew. How could you use those boots? I'm jumping to him. And I'm following, you know, his-- his boots.

speaker: interviewee
text: People, friends of mine are telling me, an SS man passes by when I was taking off the shoes from the dead body. And he points the gun. He takes out the gun from the holster, and he points the gun to my head. And the other one says to him, hey Hansel! [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: Hansel, what are you doing? He wants to have only his shoes. Let him-- leave him alone! So he takes his gun. He puts it back in his holster. And that much, what made a difference between to be or not to be. I'm taking the shoes, and wherever we stopped, I put them on. And it was 100%, 1,000% improvement. So, Mauthausen. We're coming back now to Mauthausen. Mauthausen was-- there were barracks, but they were occupied, you know, from before with inmates. But here, it was nothing. It was the free sky. Not even a roof. Nothing at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: And raining. Misery. I had some blankets. I made myself some kind of a roof over my head. It was raining. It was leaking down, so I tried, you know, to push out the water on the other side, on the other corner. And laying down in the water. Sleeping in the water.

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle lives now in Israel. He's about close to 90. My mother's younger brother. Came also to Mauthausen there, and somebody told me, he says, your uncle is here. I went to him. I says, Adolf, do you have anything to eat or something? He just came in. He says no, I don't have nothing. Anyway, here, it was miserable. What was it like to see someone from your family? Did he have any news?

speaker: interviewee
text: He asked me if I know anything about his wife, about his children. No. He didn't know nothing because he was by forced labor camp too. Only he was in other detachments in another city and in other places. And we didn't meet each other until here.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was called a [GERMAN]. A [GERMAN] in German means, where they are bringing them all together, you know? One-- on a pile. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He says, yeah. Here, they use to bring the [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] was called a garbage can with a kind of soup made out of dried cabbage.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was like eating straw. Because that never cooked. Not never boiled. But it was something. It was warm. Here we were about three weeks. They come out on the microphone. Again, the same story. Now they are making transports now to go from here away. They didn't tell you where or what or [INAUDIBLE]. You wouldn't have known anyway. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He says, we should stay in the line. We're going to get a half of bread. Whoever-- once again, the same story. Whoever cannot walk should remain behind because they're going to take him by truck. I said, uh-uh. I am not going to go by truck. I'm going to walk. And we walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately, that was already in the beginning of April. In the beginning of April. That was around the third, fourth, fifth of April, 1945. And they-- and we are going over fields. Not on the highways, because the highways were occupied with their own armies retreating from everywhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or going to the battle line, or retreating. You know, they were [NON-ENGLISH], you know? They didn't know what they are doing. But the Jew to be killed, yes. That's what they're doing. They were all-- they had a-- a knowledge about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, as we are going, so there were people, you know, they fell down. It was a friend of mine. And we had to pass over the [INAUDIBLE] river railway bridge. Because, like I say, we were going over field, over little ways, not highways. And we are going, and the guy cannot go no more. So they didn't want to leave him here behind because trucks couldn't come so much here. And we crossed under a railway bridge. He says to us, lay him on the grass. Lay him on the grass. OK. Four people should grab him. Two-- two. One on each hand, and one on each leg. And as we should schlep him with us. Now, going over the railway tracks, you know, hitting his head, you know, on the railway tracks. We were-- we came in the middle of the bridge. Says, now throw-- throw him in the water. He was still alive. Throw him in the water. So we did. And we continued. After three days we arrived into Gunskirchen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I am sure Margaret told you about Gunskirchen. We were liberated there. But here, we were also about three weeks. Here, it was nothing to do. There were new barracks. Straw on the floor. Not-- there was no floor. It was earth. But it was, about living quarters, it was 1,000% improvement from Mauthausen. Here, they were giving us a soup in the morning, and that's it. And some bread. 18 people to a bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: So whatever you were crumbling the bread, and the way you were cutting it, it crumbled. So some people had a handful of crumbs. And that was your ration bread of the day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here, I thought to myself, I am going to make myself useful. I smelled around the kitchen. Maybe they want I should chop wood for them. It's going to fall out, something from the kitchen. No. It was a place there, because it was occupied with smarter people than I was. I am going to carry dead bodies, they-- not because I wanted to. It was about two kilometers in the woods. Because this was in the woods. The camp was in the woods. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] ordered us, again, dead bodies. Because in the morning, you had to clean out the barracks, the dead-- the cadavers out, in the Totenplatz. That was in the dead place, where the dead people are. So we used to put them out. And I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] went to work here with the intention that they're going to give us a ration of-- another ration of bread. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Two kilometers, you had to schlep a dead body. That was taking out from you everything. All the calories, what you ate ten years ago. So we were working like that. And sometimes I fell in to bring water to the kitchen in the [INAUDIBLE] attachment. It was also about a kilometer and a half. We had to go with big pails. Not-- not easy to schlep the water into the camp to the kitchen. It-- we are working for a while. And then, again, I fell in another-- in the detachment-- in the dead body detachment. Until May-- Friday, May the 5th, 1945.
